coredata.dc:title: Sulfonated graphene oxide modified polysulfone-polyamide forward osmosis membrane and its application in fluorine-containing wastewater treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Jie
Jing, Weiqi
Liu, Eryong
Du, Shuangming
Cai, Hui
Du, Huiling
Wang, Jinlei
coredata.dc:description: In view of the low water flux and fluoride ion (F−) retention rate of forward osmosis emergency water bags in coal mine emergency rescue, sulfonated graphene oxide (SGO) is selected to modify the polysulfone-polyamide forward osmosis (FO) membrane to obtain high water flux and F−retention rate in this work. The results show that the crosslinking of m-phenylenediamine (MPD) improves the sulfonated graphene oxide (SGO) dispersion of the polyamide layer in the interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction, and the polyamide active layer with more hydrophilic and more water flux is obtained, and the increase in water flux is up to 6 times. Furthermore, the negatively charged SGO reduces the surface potential of the polyamide membrane, which significantly improves the F−retention effect of the high water flux FO membrane to 83.2 %. At the same time, the existence of Donnan's law leads to differences in the retention performance of different anions. Therefore, SGO-modified polysulfone-polyamide FO membrane presents excellent permeability performance, which provides a new combination of materials and technologies for fluorine-containing wastewater environmental emergency rescue and high-fluorine wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Forward osmosis membrane
Sulfonated graphene oxide
Emergency rescue
High fluorine wastewater
Permeability

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: key research and development project of Shaanxi Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Research and Development Projects of Shaanxi Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Open Project of Key Laboratory of New Marine Materials and Applied Technology of Chinese Academy of Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: All the reagents used in this paper are purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.(China), polysulfone (PSF, AR) as a support layer polymer uniformly coated on polyester screen (180 mesh), 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, AR) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, AR) are used to dissolve PSF polymer, of which polyester screen is purchased from Hangzhou Daheng Filter Cloth Co., Ltd.As shown in Fig. 5, the cross-sectional morphology of the modified film prepared by scanning electron microscopy is observed. Fig. 5a shows that the orinigal membrane (TFC) is composed of two structures: polysulfone support layer and polyamide film active layer, both the original membrane and the modified membrane have a dense PA layer on the support layer [25]. Among them, Fig. 5c is a low magnification diagram of Fig. 5a, and it is observed that the water channel size of the polysulfone film layer is about 1–3.2 μm, showing a loose finger-like structure, and the active layer of the polyamide film is distributed on the surface of the polysulfone film in granular form. Fig. 5b is the cross-sectional morphology of TFC-SGO modified membrane, comparing the water channel of TFC polysulfone layer, and it is found that there is no obvious gap, indicating that SGO only changes the polyamide layer structure, and the support layer structure is intact [34]. Combined with Fig. 5d, we observe that there is a clear active interlayer channel between the TFC-SGO membrane layers, and the thickness of the active layer is higher than that of the TFC membrane, according to previous reports, the thicker the active layer, the better its hydrophilicity, indicating that the water flux of the modified membrane is expected to increase.This work is supported by the key research and development project of Shaanxi Province [Grant No. 2021SF-469], the Open Project of Key Laboratory of New Marine Materials and Applied Technology of Chinese Academy of Sciences [Grant No. 2018K01] and the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No.51705415].

This work is supported by the key research and development project of Shaanxi Province [Grant No. 2021SF-469 ], the Open Project of Key Laboratory of New Marine Materials and Applied Technology of Chinese Academy of Sciences [Grant No. 2018K01 ] and the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 51705415 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Infrared images of (a)GO and SGO powder, (b) TFC-SGO modified membranes.
XPS diagram of TFC and TFC-SGO1 membranes (a) Full spectrum diagram, (b) S2p spectrum, (c)O1S spectrum, (d)C1s spectrum of TFC film, (e) C1s spectrum of TFC-SGO1 film.
Raman spectra of TFC membrane and TFC-SGO modified membranes: (a) Full spectrum diagram, (b) Spectra of 2D peaks.
SEM images of the surface of TFC membranes and modified membranes with different content: (a)TFC, (b)TFC-GO1, (c)TFC-SGO1, (d)TFC-SGO2, (e)TFC-SGO3.

Cross-sectional morphology of TFC and TFC-SGO modified membranes: (a)Low magnification image of TFC membrane, (b) Low magnification image of TFC-SGO membrane, (c) High magnification image of TFC membrane (d) High magnification image of TFC-SGO membrane.
Surface roughness: (a) TFC, (b) TFC-GO1, (c) TFC-SGO1, (d) TFC-SGO2, (e) TFC-SGO3, (e) Ra diagram.
Contact angle of TFC membrane and modified membranes with different content.
Water flux of TFC membrane and modified membranes with different content.
The interception rate of sulfate ion and fluoride ion.
(a) The reverse salt flux, (b)Js/Jw of TFC membrane and membrane with different content of modified materials.
Content of tunnel water before and after treatment.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: ND indicates that the content of the substance is very low and exceeds the internal measurement range of the instrument.

head.ce:title: Sulfonated graphene oxide modified polysulfone-polyamide forward osmosis membrane and its application in fluorine-containing wastewater treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi'an University of Science and Technology, Xi'an, 710054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Xi'an University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi'an University of Science and Technology, Xi'an, 710054, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In view of the low water flux and fluoride ion (F−) retention rate of forward osmosis emergency water bags in coal mine emergency rescue, sulfonated graphene oxide (SGO) is selected to modify the polysulfone-polyamide forward osmosis (FO) membrane to obtain high water flux and F−retention rate in this work. The results show that the crosslinking of m-phenylenediamine (MPD) improves the sulfonated graphene oxide (SGO) dispersion of the polyamide layer in the interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction, and the polyamide active layer with more hydrophilic and more water flux is obtained, and the increase in water flux is up to 6 times. Furthermore, the negatively charged SGO reduces the surface potential of the polyamide membrane, which significantly improves the F−retention effect of the high water flux FO membrane to 83.2 %. At the same time, the existence of Donnan's law leads to differences in the retention performance of different anions. Therefore, SGO-modified polysulfone-polyamide FO membrane presents excellent permeability performance, which provides a new combination of materials and technologies for fluorine-containing wastewater environmental emergency rescue and high-fluorine wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Coal is widely exploited as an important part of fossil fuels, but frequent mining disasters in the mining process are easy to cause major safety hazards, mine water has become an important guarantee for the life of trapped underground people, however, mine water containing excessive toxic elements can't be directly drunk. In order to solve this problem, we tested the main elements in the mine water of Shendong Coal Mine, combined with GB3838-2002 ″Surface Water Environmental Quality Standard", and found that sulfate and fluoride ions are the main pollutants exceeding the standard in mine water, as shown in Table 1 . Among them, the fluoride ion concentration is as high as 4.64 mg/L, far exceeding the fluoride ion limit concentration in national drinking water [1]. At the same time, if the concentration of fluoride ions in the human body exceeds 3 mg/L, it will cause fluorosis, leading to shock [2], so it is urgent to remove fluoride from mine water. At present, the commonly used methods for treating fluorine-containing wastewater include precipitation adsorption [2], membrane separation [3,4], ion exchange resin [5,6], electrochemical [7] and so on. Membrane separation technology is widely used because of its ability to accurately control and treat trace elements in wastewater, and can be divided into electrodialysis [8], forward osmosis [9,10], reverse osmosis [11], ultrafiltration [12] and microfiltration [13] according to different solution permeation methods, among which the forward osmosis (FO) is the method to flow water through a semi-permeable membrane from a feed solution with a lower osmotic pressure to a draw solution with a higher osmotic pressure without the need for an external driving force, allowing water molecules to pass through and trap other ions. However, low water flux and high anti-salt flux are the main problems faced by the application of forward osmosis in water treatment [10], but the introduction of nanomaterials provides new ideas to solve this problem. The researchers found that by embedding nanomaterials in polyamide active (PA) layers, the intrinsic properties of the membrane can be altered and properties such as hydrophilicity and selectivity of the membrane can be improved. Commonly used nanomaterials today include carbon-based nanomaterials (graphene, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots) and inorganic nanoparticles (carbon nitride, silica, and titanium oxide) [14,15].

In particular, graphene oxide (GO) has received extensive attention in membrane separation applications because it is rich in hydrophilic functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and epoxy groups, and the incorporation of GO has been reported to greatly improve the hydrophilicity as well as alkali resistance, fouling resistance and catalytic activity of FO membranes [16–18]. Hasan et al. [19] prepared a high-performance FO membrane through layer-by-layer composite protochitosan and graphene oxide as active layer modifiers. Wang et al. [20] prepared a composite nanohybrid membrane by organically combining SGO and ZrO2, and found that the membrane had a good adsorption and removal effect on fluoride ions (F−) in industrial wastewater, and if the F− concentration was lower than 200 mg/L, the removal rate could reach 96–99 %. Therefore, the FO membrane modification of GO as a nano-additive can improve the permeability and F− separation performance of the composite FO membrane, and can further improve the performance and expand the application field based on this material [21].

Preliminary studies have shown that simply adding GO may easily cause it to fall off on the FO membrane and cannot be uniformly dispersed in the active layer [22]. As the researchers explored further, it was found that the preparation of sulfonated graphene oxide (SGO) by grafting sulfonate (-SO3H) on GO can promote the dispersion effect in the aqueous phase of MPD while improving the thermal stability of GO during interfacial polymerization [23]. Therefore, this functionalized GO material not only retains the excellent properties of the original GO, but also improves other properties of the membrane. Ganesh [24] et al. used SGO to develop polysulfone composite membranes with excellent permeability and salt retention rate, and applied them to seawater desalination. Although SGO has achieved fruitful results in the field of FO membranes, there have been no reports of applications related to high-fluoride wastewater.

Based on these, in order to effectively remove harmful ions (F−) in mine water (high-fluoride wastewater), SGO is selected to modify the polyamide active (PA) layer. In addition, we also discuss the effects of adding different content of SGO on the surface morphology, hydrophilicity and selectivity of FO membranes, and analyze the mechanism of high retention effect of composite membranes on F−. Our work has a guiding effect on improving the permeability of polyamide forward osmosis membrane, especially the high water flux is of great significance for the emergency rescue application of FO membrane.

All the reagents used in this paper are purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.(China), polysulfone (PSF, AR) as a support layer polymer uniformly coated on polyester screen (180 mesh), 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, AR) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, AR) are used to dissolve PSF polymer, of which polyester screen is purchased from Hangzhou Daheng Filter Cloth Co., Ltd.

Graphene oxide (GO, thickness: 0.55–1.2 nm, diameter: 0.5–3 μm, number of layers: <3) is used as a raw material for the preparation of sulfonated graphene oxide (SGO), purchased from the Chengdu Institute of Organic Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences (China). Sodium dodecyl benzenesulfonate (SDBS, AR) and sodium lignosulfonate (LS, AR) provide sulfonate (-SO3H) for GO and promote GO dispersion.

Triphthalyl chloride (TMC, AR) and m-phenylenediamine (MPD, AR) are dissolved in n-hexane (n-hexane, 98 %) and deionized water for interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction, respectively, sodium hypochlorite (NaClO, AR) and sodium bisulfite (NaHSO3, AR) are used to flush excess organic matter on the membrane surface, and sodium chloride (NaCl, AR), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, AR) and sodium fluoride (NaF, AR) are used for performance tests such as FO water flux and salt retention rate.

Polysulfone membranes are prepared by phase conversion. First, dissolve PSF (12 wt%) in DMF (22 wt%) and NMP (66 wt%) mixed solution at 95 °C and stir for 8 h to obtain a homogeneous plating solution. Then, the prepared membrane casting solution is evenly coated on the polyester screen with a 150 μm scraper, and then put into water for phase conversion for 24 h. Finally, it is taken out to dry, and the prepared polysulfone membrane is stored in a crisper bag and recorded as TFC.

Dispensing 40 g/L of SDBS aqueous solution and take out 50 mL for backup; 0.2 g GO and 2.7 g LS mixture is added to it for 2 h ultrasound; after that, it is mechanically stirred for 8 h at a constant temperature of 80 °C; finally, the mixture is centrifuged and freeze-dried to obtain SGO dispersion. SGO (1 wt%, 2 wt%, 3 wt%) and GO (1 wt%) dispersions are added to MPD aqueous solution (3.4 wt%) for 2 h ultrasound to form MPD backup solution.

The selective PA layer is prepared on PSF substrate by IP technology. Firstly, the dried polysulfone membrane is immersed in MPD backup solution for 2 min, the excess solution on the membrane surface is scraped off with an air knife, and then immerse in 0.15 wt% TMC/n-hexane solution for 1 min. After taking out, cure under an atmosphere of 95 °C for 2 min to improve the PA crosslinking. Subsequently, 200 ppm NaClO solution and 1000 ppm NaHSO3 solution are used to rinse for 30 s, which could not only dissolve the organic matter on the membrane surface, but also effectively remove the contamination of microorganisms and make the membrane flux higher. Finally, it is cured in deionized water at 95 °C to obtain a uniform flat sulfongraphene-modified polysulfone-polyamide composite (TFC-SGO) membrane. The membrane is dried and stored in a crisper bag, which is recorded as TFC-SGO1, TFC-SGO2, TFC-SGO3, TFC-GO1, respectively.

GO and SGO are characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, NICOLET 6700, Thermo, USA), and functional groups with wavenumbers of 100–3250 cm−1 are analyzed to verify whether sulfonic acid groups are adsorbed on GO. Raman spectroscopy (Raman, inVia Reflex, USA) is used to analyze the active layer components and confirm whether SGO is confirmed introduced into the PA active layer of the modified membrane. To further demonstrate the stability of SGO within the membrane, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Utltra DLD, Kratos, UK) is used to analyze the chemical composition of the membrane at a depth of about 2–5 nm.

The surface and cross-sectional morphology of the membrane is observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss Gemini500, USA). Atomic force microscopy (AFM, SPM-9700HT, Germany) is used to analyze the surface roughness of membranes for quantitative and qualitative analysis. Water contact angle and hydrophilicity are measured by a contact angle measuring instrument (DACT21, Dataphysics, Germany), and all data are calculated as an average of at least 5 points.

The performance of forward osmosis membranes is characterized by measuring water flux, salt retention rate, and anti-salt flux. Deionized water and 2 mol/L NaCl solution are used as feedstock solution (FS) and draw solution (DS), respectively, and this measurement is used to test the magnitude of the water flux, and its equation (Jw, LM−2H−1, LMH) is shown in Equation (1). SO4 2− and F− in coal mine roadway water are simulated by 1 g/L MgSO4 solution and 0.2 g/L NaF solution, and glucose is used as the extraction solution to calculate the salt retention rate of the forward osmosis membrane, as shown in Equation (2). We record the concentration and volume changes of the solution over time and measure the anti-salt flux of SO4 2−(Js, mol m−2h−1, mMH) using equation (3) [25].
(1)
J W
=
Δ V
Δ t
1 A
where
Δ V
is the volume change of the stock solution (L);
Δ t
is the penetration experiment test time (h);
A
is the effective area of the membrane (m2).
(2)
R = ( 1 ‐
C p
C f
) × 100 %
where
R
is the retention rate;
C p
is the salt concentration (g/L) of the extract;
C f
is the salt concentration (g/L) of the feed solution.
(3)
J s
=
C t
V t
−
C 0
V 0
Δ t
1 A
where
C 0
and
V 0
are salt concentration (g/L) and volume (L) at the beginning of the osmotic experiment, respectively;
C t
and
V t
are salt concentration (g/L) and volume (L) at the end of the osmotic experiment, respectively;
Δ t
is the penetration experiment test time (h);
A
is the effective area of the membrane (m2).

FTIR spectroscopy is used to verify the sulfonation results of graphene oxide, as shown in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1a shows the infrared spectra of GO particles and SGO particles. The comparison between the two shows that the peaks of SGO at 1092 cm−1 and 1730 cm−1 are significantly weakened, indicating that the CO in the carboxyl group and the C–O–C in the epoxy group of GO are consumed during sulfonation, and at 1038 cm−1, 1207 cm−1 and 1261 cm−1, respectively, there are tensile vibration peaks of SO, symmetrical tensile vibration peaks of OSO and asymmetric tensile vibration peaks of OSO, The presence of these characteristic peaks of sulfonic acid roots (-SO3H) indicates the introduction of sulfonic acid groups to the GO surface [26]. Although the characteristic peaks change, the CC (635 cm−1) and –OH (3432 cm−1) absorption peaks share with GO can still be observed on the SGO surface, which means that the SGO nanosheets retain their original graphene oxide properties even after sulfonation treatment [27].

For further exploring the effect of sulfonated graphene oxide on the active layer of polyamide, we perform infrared tests on the original membrane and its modified film, as shown in Fig. 1b. The results show that the characteristic peak of –COO– (ester group) at 1715 cm−1 and the absorption peak of –CONH– (amide bond) at 1601 cm−1 prove that the PA active layer is synthesized on the substrate by IP reaction [19,27]. Compared with the original FO membrane, the modified membrane shows a significant peak at 3340 cm−1, representing the telescopic vibration peak of –OH, and the peak width is large, indicating the content of –OH is high, and the peak intensity also increases significantly with the increase of SGO addition. Since SGO contains more –OH groups [27], it is further confirmed that SGO is successfully introduced into the PA active layer.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to analyze the composition and chemical state of the original and modified films, as shown in Fig. 2 . XPS shows that elements C, O and N appear in the original film, while S elements are only present in TFC-SGO modified films, again indicating the successful introduction of sulfonate [28]. From Fig. 2b, the binding energy of sp2 is 168.5 eV, while the binding energy of element S is 165 eV, indicating that S exists in the form –SO3H. Comparing the C1s peak plots of Fig. 2(d) and (e), C1s corrects +1.07 V, indicating that the C1s spectrum contains peaks associate with C–C (284.17 eV), C–O (286.17 eV), and CO (288.19 eV) [29].

Fig. 3 shows the Raman spectra of the original membrane and the modified membrane with different SGO additions. As shown in Fig. 3a, the modified membrane with different SGO additions has obvious peaks at 1239 cm−1, 1587 cm−1 and 2588 cm−1, corresponding to D peak, G peak and 2D peak, respectively. Among them, the D peak represents the defect of the carbon lattice in the graphene derivative, and the greater the D peak strength, the more defects there are [30], it can be seen that the D peak change is not obvious, indicating that SGO is well introduced and has good stability in the PA layer; The G peak represents the in-plane tensile vibration of sp2 hybrid carbon [31], and with the increase of SGO addition, the height of G peak gradually increases, and the sp2 vibration gradually increases, representing that SGO and PA layer have achieved a functionalized combination. The 2D peak is approximately equal to twice the D peak in numerical terms, and the change of its position and half-peak width helps us accurately calculate the number of graphene layers, and compared with the 2D peak sub-peak and layer number change rule, as shown in Fig. 3b, it can be known that the SGO layer number is 1–2 layers, indicating that after sulfonation modification, the GO layer number will not change.

The surface morphology of polysulfone-polyamide original membrane and different SGO addition modified films is shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a shows the surface morphology of the original polyamide membrane, a dense distribution can be observed with a typical peak-valley structure that provides a water channel for the infiltration of water during forward osmosis. Fig. 4b shows a modified membrane with 1 wt% GO added, and it is observed that the "peak-valley" structure on the surface of the original membrane is covered by irregular ultra-thin GO nanosheets with folded structures, and the GO nanosheets are severely agglomerate, which leads to the blockage of the water channel [32], thereby reducing the effective area and water flux. When SGO is added, the surface fold evolves into a uniform network structure, and with the increase of SGO content, a looser and uniformly distributed molecular layer is formed above the PA layer, which helps to improve the porosity of the modified membrane, thereby increasing the water flux of the membrane, which is consistent with the report of Wang et al. [33] At the same time, the layered structure of SGO makes the diffusion path of solute molecules more tortuous, thereby improving the salt resistance of the modified film.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the cross-sectional morphology of the modified film prepared by scanning electron microscopy is observed. Fig. 5a shows that the orinigal membrane (TFC) is composed of two structures: polysulfone support layer and polyamide film active layer, both the original membrane and the modified membrane have a dense PA layer on the support layer [25]. Among them, Fig. 5c is a low magnification diagram of Fig. 5a, and it is observed that the water channel size of the polysulfone film layer is about 1–3.2 μm, showing a loose finger-like structure, and the active layer of the polyamide film is distributed on the surface of the polysulfone film in granular form. Fig. 5b is the cross-sectional morphology of TFC-SGO modified membrane, comparing the water channel of TFC polysulfone layer, and it is found that there is no obvious gap, indicating that SGO only changes the polyamide layer structure, and the support layer structure is intact [34]. Combined with Fig. 5d, we observe that there is a clear active interlayer channel between the TFC-SGO membrane layers, and the thickness of the active layer is higher than that of the TFC membrane, according to previous reports, the thicker the active layer, the better its hydrophilicity, indicating that the water flux of the modified membrane is expected to increase.

Fig. 6 shows AFM images of the original and modified membrane, and the average roughness parameter (Ra) is also shown. First, polyamide films consist of nanoscale particles with relatively flat surfaces. With the addition of GO, Fig. 6b shows that a small amount of GO agglomeration occurs in the polyamide layer and the particle size of polyamide decreases, which is why the roughness of adding GO decreases. With the addition of SGO, Fig. 6c–e shows that the particle size of the surface of the TFC-SGO modified membrane is increasing, and pores are easy to form between large particles, and the presence of these pores will provide additional water channels, indicating that the introduction of SGO contributes to the formation of larger and denser pores, thereby increasing water flux, which is consistent with the results of SEM [32,35]. It can be seen from the Ra data that the roughness of the membrane increases with the adding of SGO, which is conducive to the enhancement of water permeability [19,36].

Fig. 7 shows the water contact angle (CA), which is much lower than that of TFC-SGO membrane (48 ± 1.5°) than TFC membrane (85.5 ± 3°), due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups such as sulfonate and hydroxyl groups in SGO, which enhances the hydrophilicity of the modified membrane. High hydrophilicity is more conducive to improving the adsorption of water molecules, so that water molecules enter the membrane pores, which is convenient for the transport of water in the membrane. In conclusion, small and uniform SGO nanosheets in the PA layer may help to improve the permeability of the PA active layer, which is consistent with the water flux test results, means that the FO membrane can be both permeable and selective by SGO modification.

Water flux is one of the main indicators to test FO performance, the quality of hydrophilicity directly determines the size of water flux, the smaller the membrane contact angle, the better the hydrophilicity, the greater the water flux. According to Fig. 8 , all modified membranes exhibite higher water fluxes than the original membranes, with a high water flux of 3 wt% SGO reaching 42 L m−2 h−1, which is 6 times higher than the original membrane, directly verifies the increased porosity observed by SEM and AFM.

In order to further test the FO performance of the original membrane and the modified membrane, the retention effect of the modified membrane on salt ions is first explored, and the fluoride ion with excessive content in mine water is used as the research index, and sulfate ion is selected for comparison, the separation performance of the composite membrane and the original forward osmosis membrane under different SGO addition amounts is measured, and the specific retention rate test results are shown in Fig. 9 . Among them, the retention rates of F− and SO4 2− of the original membrane are 78.0 % and 88.5 %, respectively. With the addition of SGO, the retention rate of modified membranes for SO4 2− decreases, while the retention rate of F− increases, up to 83.2 %. The evolution of this high fluorine retention is greatly affected by the action of membrane surface and electrolyte charge, because the SGO modified film is negatively charged and can hinder the osmosis of polyvalent ions by electrostatic action [37]. In the case of FO membranes, the monovalent anions are first intercepted, and then the polyvalent anions, so F− is first intercepted. However, according to Donnan's law, due to the excessive rejection of F−, the concentration balance of the membrane is disrupted, resulting in a decrease in the retention performance of the modified membrane for SO4 2− [24].

The anti-salt flux and Js/Jw are selected to analyze the permeability of polysulfone-polyamide forward osmosis membranes. The lower the anti-salt flux, the lower the tendency of the FO membrane to foul. According to the results of Fig. 10 a, the antifouling performance of the modified membrane is significantly improved with the addition of SGO, and the anti-salt flux of TFC-SGO3 membrane is less than 0.06 MH, which confirms the excellent permeability of polysulfone-polyamide positive permeable membrane. In addition, the lower the Js/Jw value, the higher the selectivity of the membrane. As shown in Fig. 10b, the Js/Jw of the polyamide film also decreases rapidly through the modification of SGO, and the Js/Jw value of TFC-SGO3 is lower than 0.003, indicating that the SGO nanolayer makes the salt ion diffusion path more tortuous and effectively improves the salt retenion effect. In summary, the stable reduction of anti-salt flux and Js/Jw data show that the SGO-modified TFC-SGO3 membrane has excellent synergistic effect [38].

In our work, SGO is doped into the polyamide layer by traditional IP method and a TFC-SGO modified composite membrane with high hydrophilicity and F− retention rate is prepared. The doping of SGO nanomaterials can improve the hydrophilicity of the FO membrane and form a loose fold structure, thereby obtaining a high F− retention rate. At the same time, under the influence of Donnan equilibrium, the retention effect of its competing ions (SO4 2−) is reduced, showing good synergy of permeability and selectivity, and this modified composite membrane provides a reference for the removal of fluoride ions in high fluoride wastewater, which has guiding significance for the modification of FO membrane.

Jie Yu: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Weiqi Jing: Data curation, Formal analysis. Eryong Liu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Shuangming Du: Supervision. Hui Cai: Validation. Huiling Du: Resources. Jinlei Wang: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the key research and development project of Shaanxi Province [Grant No. 2021SF-469], the Open Project of Key Laboratory of New Marine Materials and Applied Technology of Chinese Academy of Sciences [Grant No. 2018K01] and the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No.51705415].

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The effect of Fe-Mn minerals and seawater interface and enrichment mechanism of ore-forming elements of polymetallic crusts and nodules from the South China Sea
Acta Oceanol. Sin.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Yao, W. Chi, S. Xiaoming, J. Xiaodong, S.A. Rina, Z. Li, H. Yi, L. Yating, L.I. Xiaojie, L.U. Rongfei, The effect of Fe-Mn minerals and seawater interface and enrichment mechanism of ore-forming elements of polymetallic crusts and nodules from the South China Sea, Acta Oceanologica Sinica 36(006) (2017) 34-46.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improved thin-film-composite forward-osmosis membrane for coal mine water purification
Mater. Chem. Phys.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Liu, W. Jing, X. Zhang, S. Du, Z. Zeng, L. Zhu, H. Du, Improved thin-film-composite forward-osmosis membrane for coal mine water purification, Materials Chemistry and Physics 283 (2022) 126011-.
